13.display: 10 trials is too small a sample — random variation means the result can be far from the true probability, which is actually 0.5.

14.display: Sometimes — relative frequency can be close to or even equal to the true probability by chance, but it is generally only an estimate, especially with fewer trials.

15.display: Yes — with more trials, relative frequency tends to settle closer to the true probability.

16.display: No — 6 rolls is far too small a sample to prove anything about the true probability; it only gives a rough estimate.

17.display: The student with 500 flips — larger samples generally give more reliable estimates of the true probability.

18.display: False — relative frequency is always between 0 and 1 (or 0% and 100%), since it can never be more occurrences than trials.

19.display: No — a fair dice has a theoretical probability of 1/6 (about 0.167) for rolling a 6, so a relative frequency close to 0.3 suggests this dice may be biased.

21.display: P(scores) = 18/30 = 0.6; predicted scoring matches out of 10 = 6

22.display: P(win) estimate = 125/500 = 1/4 = 0.25, which matches the theoretical 1/4 for 4 equal sections, so the spinner appears fair.

23.display: Yes, it appears fair — every relative frequency (e.g. 48/300, 52/300...) is close to 1/6 (about 0.167), with only small natural variation.

26.display: Probability = 51/60 = 0.85; predicted germinating seeds out of 500 = 425

27.display: For example, flipping a fair coin just twice could easily give two heads (relative frequency 1.0), even though the true probability is 0.5 — small samples are very sensitive to chance.

28.display: A sensible design using many repeats (e.g. 100 or more trials), stating that a relative frequency far from the expected theoretical probability would suggest bias.

29.display: Relative frequency = 150/1000 = 0.15, which is close to the claimed 1/5 = 0.2, so the result broadly supports the claim, though it is slightly lower.

30.display: Theoretical probability is calculated from known outcomes without doing an experiment (e.g. P(heads) = 1/2 from reasoning about a fair coin); experimental (relative frequency) probability is estimated by actually repeating a trial and counting results (e.g. testing how often a spinner lands on red).
